affected_groups: Disadvantaged and low-SES pupils whose access to comprehensive schooling depends on enforced anti-sorting rules
Teachers, especially in rural and STEM subjects facing acute shortages, who must deliver mixed-ability differentiation without adequate retraining capacity
Middle-class and higher-SES families who may respond to structural delay by intensifying school-choice and catchment-based sorting strategies
Employers and the digital/STEM skills pipeline, which could suffer from a multi-year instructional-quality dip during the transition
content.decision_question: Equity through structural reform versus the practical risk that, without enforced complementary rules, the reform merely relocates sorting into school choice and catchment channels — i.e. substantive equity versus paper equity
content.tension: Delaying academic tracking to age 14+ (comprehensive lower-secondary phase) addresses the root sorting mechanism and is supported by strong cross-national evidence (Poland +0.25-0.28 SD, Finland reduced income-elasticity), but its equity gain depends entirely on simultaneously enacted and enforced anti-sorting rules for school choice and catchment.
content.title: Decision tension A7: S3
value_poles: the practical risk that, without enforced complementary rules, the reform merely relocates sorting into school choice and catchment channels — i.e. substantive equity versus paper equity
